era_context: The 12th century CE was marked by the Crusades, the consolidation of feudal kingdoms in Europe, the height of the Seljuk Empire in Anatolia and the Levant, the Song Dynasty's rule in China (now confined to the south after 1127), and the rise of the Khmer Empire in Southeast Asia. In the Islamic world, the Atabeg system was fragmenting regional power. This was an era of significant cultural production: Romanesque architecture, Islamic scholarship, and Chinese landscape painting flourished.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Eastern Europe and the Rus' principalities

events.title: Holy Roman Emperor Lothair II — Conflict with the Papacy and Italian city-states
events.description: Holy Roman Emperor Lothair II (r. 1125–1137) was engaged in ongoing conflicts with the Papacy and Italian city-states over imperial authority and ecclesiastical independence. In 1133, Lothair II was consolidating his power in Germany and Italy, but faced resistance from the Welfs (Guelphs) and other German princes who opposed his centralising policies. The conflict between the Holy Roman Empire and the Papacy over investiture and secular authority remained a defining feature of this period. Lothair II's reign was marked by military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations to maintain imperial authority.
events.certainty_note: Lothair II's reign and his conflicts with the Papacy are well-documented in contemporary chronicles. The specific events of 1133 are less detailed than major political crises.

events.title: King Fulk of Jerusalem — Consolidation of Crusader authority in the Levant
events.description: King Fulk of Jerusalem (r. 1131–1143) was consolidating his authority over the Kingdom of Jerusalem and the Crusader States. Fulk had married Melisende, the daughter of King Baldwin II, and was establishing himself as a strong military and political leader. In 1133, Fulk was engaged in military campaigns against Muslim-held territories and internal consolidation of Crusader authority. His reign was marked by the construction of fortifications and the organisation of the Crusader military order.

sources.name: Fulcher of Chartres, History of the Expedition to Jerusalem

events.certainty_note: Fulk's reign and his consolidation of Crusader authority are well-documented in Crusader chronicles. The specific events of 1133 are less detailed than major battles or sieges.
cross_references: 1131-001 (Fulk's accession to the throne of Jerusalem)

events.title: Song Dynasty — Emperor Qinzong's early reign; fiscal and military challenges
events.description: Emperor Qinzong of the Song Dynasty (r. 1125–1127) was in the early years of his reign in 1133. Qinzong had ascended to the throne in 1125 following his father Huizong's abdication. His reign was marked by continued fiscal reforms and increasing military pressure from the Jurchen Jin Dynasty in the north. The Song state was at the height of its economic power but faced significant external threats. Qinzong's reign would be cut short by the Jurchen invasion of 1127, which forced the Song court to flee south and established the Southern Song Dynasty.

sources.name: Official Song government records and edicts
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Qinzong's reign is well-documented in Song official records. The specific state of affairs in 1133 is less detailed than major political events.
cross_references: 1127-001 (Jurchen invasion and fall of Northern Song)

events.title: Khmer Empire — Suryavarman II's reign; Angkor Wat construction continues
events.description: King Suryavarman II of the Khmer Empire (r. 1113–1150) continued his reign during a period of significant architectural and cultural achievement. Angkor Wat, the massive temple complex dedicated to Vishnu, was under active construction. The Khmer state was at the height of its territorial extent and economic power, controlling much of Southeast Asia. Suryavarman II's reign was marked by military campaigns against neighbouring kingdoms and the consolidation of Khmer cultural and religious authority.

sources.name: Inscriptions on Angkor Wat temple walls

events.certainty_note: Suryavarman II's reign is well-attested in epigraphic evidence and archaeological remains. The specific events of 1133 are less detailed in surviving sources.

events.title: Seljuk Empire — Zengi's consolidation of power in Syria and Mesopotamia
events.description: Imad ad-Din Zengi, the Seljuk Atabeg (military governor), continued his consolidation of power in Syria and Mesopotamia. By 1133, Zengi had secured control of Aleppo (captured in 1128) and was expanding his authority over fragmented Seljuk and Crusader-held territories. His strategy of unifying Muslim-held lands against the Crusaders would influence his son Nur ad-Din and ultimately Saladin. Zengi's reign marked the beginning of the Zengid dynasty and a shift toward centralised Islamic military power in the Levant.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)

events.certainty_note: Zengi's consolidation of power is well-documented in Islamic chronicles. The specific events of 1133 are less detailed than major military campaigns.

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the characterisation of Lothair II's reign as marked by 'consolidation' is somewhat oversimplified—his reign was marked by significant conflicts and instability, particularly with German princes and the Papacy.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1133 is moderate in Europe and the Islamic world, but sparser for East Asia and Southeast Asia beyond official records and archaeological evidence. Western sources are biased toward imperial and papal perspectives; Islamic sources provide crucial counterbalance. The Khmer Empire's history relies heavily on epigraphic evidence and later accounts. The specific events of 1133 are less detailed in surviving sources than major political crises or military campaigns.

graph_edges.note: Lothair II's conflicts with the Papacy continued until his death in 1137
Fulk's consolidation of Crusader authority continued until his death in 1143
Qinzong's reign was cut short by the Jurchen invasion in 1127
Suryavarman II's reign continued until his death in 1150
